explicit_text: I was born in Dixon, near Sacramento, on December 24, 1873. Our family were farmers working in the wheat fields there. It was my early home before we moved away in 1876 to Shasta County.
implicit_text: I was born in a small farming town near Sacramento on December 24, 1873. Our family worked in the wheat fields there. It was my early home before we moved away in 1876 to a more northern county.

explicit_text: Dixon, where I was born, is near Sacramento, California. Although I didn’t live in Sacramento itself, it was the closest major city to my birthplace. My family’s early life was connected to the agricultural surroundings of that area.
implicit_text: The town where I was born is close to the capital city of California. Although I didn’t live in that city itself, it was the nearest major urban center to my birthplace. My family’s early life was connected to the agricultural surroundings of that region.

explicit_text: In 1876, when I was about three years old, my family moved up to Shasta County. We settled on Cow Creek, about six miles east of Anderson, near Redding. This was where I lost my sight in 1881, and where my father worked hauling lumber to the railroad at Anderson.
implicit_text: In 1876, when I was about three years old, my family moved up to a northern California county known for its forests. We settled on Cow Creek, about six miles east of a small town near Redding. This was where I lost my sight in 1881, and where my father worked hauling lumber to the railroad at that nearby town.

explicit_text: Anderson was the town near where we lived in Shasta County. My father hauled lumber from the sawmills in the mountains down to the railroad at Anderson. It was part of our daily life and work during my childhood before I went blind.
implicit_text: The town near where we lived in that northern county was a hub for the railroad. My father hauled lumber from the mountain sawmills down to the railroad there. It was part of our daily life and work during my childhood before I went blind.

explicit_text: Redding was the larger town near where we lived in Shasta County. Our home was about six miles east of Anderson, which is near Redding. The area around Redding was full of sawmills and timber work that my father was involved in.
implicit_text: The larger town near where we lived in that northern county was a center for timber and sawmills. Our home was about six miles east of the smaller town near this larger city. The area around that city was full of sawmills and timber work that my father was involved in.

explicit_text: After my father passed away in 1883, arrangements were made to send me to the School for the Blind in Berkeley, California. I arrived there in August 1883 when I was not quite ten years old. Berkeley was a very different place from the country life I had known, and it took some adjustment.
implicit_text: After my father passed away in 1883, arrangements were made to send me to a specialized school for the blind in a well-known East Bay city. I arrived there in August 1883 when I was not quite ten years old. That city was very different from the country life I had known, and it took some adjustment.

explicit_text: The School for the Blind in Berkeley was where I was educated after losing my sight. It was located on Derby Street and shared a building with the deaf children. There were about sixty blind children, and I was taught by two teachers including Mr. Charles Wilkinson. The school was a challenging but formative experience for me.

There were about sixty blind children, and I was taught by two teachers including one named Charles Wilkinson. The school was a challenging but formative experience for me.

explicit_text: Mr. Charles Wilkinson was one of my teachers at the School for the Blind in Berkeley. He taught the older blind boys and was very boyish and well-liked by all of us. He was not formally trained by today's standards but was a wonderful teacher who helped me get through what would be considered the tenth grade now. He was also the brother of the principal, Mr. Warring Wilkinson.

explicit_text: Mr. Warring Wilkinson was the principal of the School for the Blind in Berkeley and the brother of my teacher, Charles Wilkinson. He was better trained and more of an administrator than a teacher. Although he didn’t spend much time with us, he took a special interest in me, especially after I excelled in an arithmetic contest against deaf students.
